Our approach

Most organisations
are not short of data.
They are short of decisions.

We work backwards from the decision that needs to be made — defining the question before designing the answer. Strategy, engineering, and AI: each in its proper sequence, each with a clear reason for being there.

01
01
Data Strategy & Architecture
Built around how the business decides, not how data is stored. We define the data strategy before selecting the platform — connecting investment to the outcomes that matter.
02
02
AI & Analytics in Production
From concept to continuous operation at enterprise scale. We move organisations beyond proof of concept — building AI capabilities that run reliably, at volume, without special handling.
03
03
Data Engineering & Platforms
Databricks, Fabric, Snowflake, Azure, AWS — pragmatic delivery on modern infrastructure. Architecture that serves the business model, not just the technology preference.

Aviation / Airports

From reactive to predictive — operations transformed across the entire airport ecosystem.

How one high-volume airport environment gained the visibility to anticipate disruption before it formed — across queues, baggage, gates, and ground movement.

[X]%
Reduction in passenger
wait times
[X]%
Faster operational
response
[X]
Key operational areas
monitored in real time
The challenge

High-volume airport environments operate under constant pressure. Passenger surges, queue build-up, baggage delays, gate coordination issues, and ground movement bottlenecks can escalate quickly when teams rely on fragmented systems and delayed visibility.

The challenge was not only to monitor operations, but to anticipate disruption early enough to act on it. A reactive model — however well-executed — could not keep pace with the complexity and variability of a modern airport at scale.

What BI3 delivered

BI3 helped shape a more predictive operating model by combining real-time operational visibility, forecasting, and intelligent monitoring across key parts of the airport ecosystem.

This created earlier visibility into passenger movement, congestion patterns, baggage readiness, and ground-side coordination — giving teams the information they needed to respond before issues became critical, rather than after they had formed.

The solution connected previously fragmented operational signals into a unified picture that multiple teams could act from simultaneously.

Impact created

The result was a more connected view of operations, enabling faster decisions, improved flow across high-pressure areas, and more proactive planning of staff, gates, and resources.

Instead of reacting to bottlenecks after they formed, teams were better equipped to manage flow ahead of time. The shift from reactive to predictive operations changed both the speed and the confidence with which decisions could be made.

Capabilities applied
Predictive Analytics Real-Time Operational Intelligence Passenger Flow Forecasting Baggage Readiness Monitoring Ground Operations Visibility Data Platform Engineering

Passenger Feedback Intelligence

Aviation / Customer Experience / Enterprise AI

From fragmented feedback to a
continuous intelligence capability.

How a promising AI pilot was evolved into a scalable, multilingual platform for processing passenger feedback — and made part of how the organisation actually operates.

[X]+
Feedback records
analysed
[X]
Languages
supported
[X]%
Faster insight
generation
The challenge

Passenger feedback contains valuable operational and service insight, but in many environments it remains fragmented, manual to review, and difficult to scale — especially when it spans multiple languages and large data volumes.

What had already shown value in a pilot needed to become something far more robust and enterprise-ready. The organisation had proven the concept. The challenge was to productionise it: to make it reliable, scalable, and continuously operational without requiring specialist intervention every time it was used.

What BI3 delivered

BI3 evolved the use case from a promising AI initiative into a scalable platform for multilingual feedback processing, translation, classification, and insight generation.

The focus was not only model performance, but building an operating capability that could support production use, ongoing growth, and integration into broader workflows. The platform was designed to run continuously — processing new feedback as it arrived, at whatever volume was required — without manual orchestration.

This meant engineering for reliability, not just accuracy: predictable cost, automatic scaling, and model architecture that could be updated without disrupting the pipeline that depended on it.

Impact created

This enabled faster analysis of customer sentiment and service issues, improved consistency in insight extraction, and created a stronger foundation for continuous service improvement.

Rather than reviewing feedback in isolated cycles, the organisation could move toward a structured and scalable intelligence model. The shift was not just operational — it changed the relationship between feedback data and the decisions it was meant to inform.

Capabilities applied
Multilingual NLP AI Platform Engineering Feedback Classification Sentiment Analysis Scalable Cloud Architecture Production AI Delivery

Aviation / Hospitality / Customer Experience

Lounge management moved from
assumptions to live operational intelligence.

How real-time occupancy monitoring and dwell-time analytics gave lounge teams the visibility to make better decisions — at the moment those decisions mattered.

[X]%
Improvement in
space utilisation
[X]%
Better peak-time
planning accuracy
Live
Visibility across all
key lounge zones
The challenge

Lounge operations often struggle with uneven occupancy, limited visibility into passenger dwell time, and difficulty predicting pressure points across different spaces.

Without timely insight, staffing, service planning, and space management become reactive — teams respond to crowding they can see, not crowding they could have anticipated. The result is inconsistent passenger experience, inefficient resource allocation, and lost opportunity to manage premium environments with the precision they deserve.

What BI3 delivered

BI3 introduced real-time occupancy monitoring, dwell-time analytics, and spatial visibility into lounge activity. This created a clearer picture of how spaces were being used, when crowding was most likely to occur, and where operational adjustments could improve service delivery and utilisation.

The solution was designed around the decisions that lounge managers actually need to make — staffing allocation, catering timing, space management during peak periods — rather than around what was technically possible to measure. Data was made available at the moment it was useful, not in end-of-day reports.

Impact created

The solution supported better planning decisions across staffing, catering, and space usage while also improving the passenger experience. With clearer visibility into occupancy and movement patterns, teams could respond more confidently and manage lounge environments more effectively.

The broader shift was from assumption-led to evidence-led management — a change that compounded over time as teams built familiarity with the data and learned to act earlier, with greater precision.

Capabilities applied
Real-Time Occupancy Monitoring Dwell-Time Analytics Spatial Intelligence Computer Vision Operational Dashboards Predictive Resource Planning

Back to thinking
Industry Perspective

The airport of the next decade will be defined by what it can anticipate — not what it can react to.

From queue prediction to baggage readiness and lounge analytics — how AI and real-time intelligence are transforming airport operations at every level.

The operational complexity problem

A modern airport is one of the most operationally complex environments in the world. Hundreds of variables — flight schedules, passenger volumes, weather, staffing, equipment status, security throughput — interact continuously and unpredictably. When any one of them shifts, the effects ripple across the entire system.

For most of the past two decades, airport operations teams have managed this complexity reactively — using experience, communication, and real-time observation to respond to events as they occurred. That model has served the industry well. But it has a ceiling, and many high-volume airports are approaching it.

The question is not whether airports will adopt AI. It is whether they will adopt it in ways that change operational posture — or merely add another dashboard to an already crowded control room.

Where AI is creating genuine operational value

The most meaningful applications of AI in airport operations share a common characteristic: they shift the decision point earlier. Instead of responding to a queue that has already formed, a predictive model can identify the conditions likely to produce that queue — and give teams time to act.

This is happening across multiple domains simultaneously. Passenger flow forecasting uses historical patterns, flight data, and real-time sensor inputs to predict crowd surges at security, immigration, and boarding gates before they materialise. Baggage readiness monitoring tracks the status of luggage handling continuously, surfacing delays before they become aircraft-holding events. Lounge occupancy analytics give hospitality teams live visibility into how their spaces are being used — not just how many guests have entered, but how long they are staying and where pressure is building.

What makes these applications valuable is not the sophistication of the models. It is the quality of the data they operate on, the speed at which that data is made available, and the design of the interface through which operational teams access and act on the intelligence.

The infrastructure gap

Most airports already collect significant amounts of operational data. The challenge is rarely data availability — it is data quality, integration, and latency. Sensor readings that are delayed by fifteen minutes, passenger counts that are aggregated rather than granular, baggage data that sits in a separate system from gate management — these are the architectural constraints that limit what AI can do.

Building a genuinely predictive operating environment requires addressing the infrastructure layer first. That means real-time data pipelines, a unified operational data model, and the governance frameworks to ensure the data that enters these systems can be trusted.

The technology is rarely the limiting factor. The data foundation almost always is.

What the next phase looks like

The airports making the most progress are those that have treated operational AI not as a set of isolated use cases, but as a connected intelligence layer — one that spans passengers, baggage, vehicles, ground equipment, and aircraft simultaneously.

When these domains are connected, the insight available to operations teams changes qualitatively, not just quantitatively. A delay in baggage handling becomes visible in the gate management picture. A predicted passenger surge surfaces in the staffing plan. The decisions that currently require co-ordination across multiple teams begin to happen earlier — and with more shared context.

That is the direction of travel. The airports that get there first will not simply be more efficient. They will operate in a fundamentally different mode — one where the management of complexity is, to a significant degree, continuous and anticipatory rather than episodic and reactive.

See how BI3 has delivered this in practice

Our case studies explore the real-world implementation of predictive operations, lounge intelligence, and AI-powered feedback processing in high-volume aviation environments.

Predictive Airport Operations Lounge Occupancy Analytics
Back to thinking
AI Strategy

Most AI pilots succeed. Most never reach production. The gap is almost always the same.

Why scalable AI delivery matters more than isolated proof-of-concept success — and what it takes to close the distance between a promising result and a system that can be relied upon.

The proof-of-concept paradox

Enterprise AI programmes often begin the same way. A business problem is identified. A team is assembled. A proof of concept is built. The results are credible — sometimes impressive. An executive demonstration is delivered. A decision is made to proceed.

And then, in a significant proportion of cases, the programme stalls. The model that worked in the controlled environment of a pilot does not find its way into the operational environment of the organisation. The capability that demonstrated measurable value never becomes part of how the business actually operates.

This is not a niche problem. It is arguably the central challenge of enterprise AI adoption today — and it is one that does not receive enough honest attention.

A proof of concept answers one question: does this work? A production system must answer a different one: can this be relied upon, at scale, by people who did not build it?

Why pilots fail to become platforms

The reasons that AI pilots do not reach production are rarely technical in the narrow sense. The model is usually sound. The underlying insight is often genuinely valuable. The failure is almost always architectural and organisational.

A proof of concept is built to demonstrate. It is optimised for the demonstration: for showing that the model performs, that the insight is real, that the investment is justified. It is not optimised for the conditions of production: for reliability under variable input quality, for operation by people who were not involved in building it, for maintenance as the model drifts and the data changes over time.

The infrastructure required to turn a working model into a dependable system is substantial. It includes data pipeline engineering, model monitoring and retraining frameworks, access controls and audit logging, integration with the operational systems that will consume the outputs, and the governance structures that ensure the capability can be maintained and evolved without specialist intervention every time something changes.

Building that infrastructure is not glamorous. It does not produce the kind of results that get shown in executive demonstrations. But it is the difference between a capability and an experiment.

The engineering discipline that turns a model into a system is what most AI programmes underinvest in — and what most AI failures trace back to.

What the transition actually requires

Moving from AI pilot to enterprise platform requires clarity about what you are actually trying to build. Not a better model — though that may be part of it — but a capability: a system that can be operated continuously, at the volume required, with predictable cost and performance, and that can be maintained and improved over time without being rebuilt from scratch each time.

That means making architectural decisions that a proof of concept is never forced to make. How does the system handle input data that does not match the training distribution? What happens when the model needs to be updated? How are outputs consumed — as a batch export, an API, an embedded decision in an operational workflow? What does failure look like, and how is it detected?

These are not questions that can be deferred until after the pilot succeeds. They shape the architecture of the platform from the beginning. Organisations that treat them as afterthoughts consistently find themselves rebuilding rather than deploying.

The right moment to make the investment

The organisations that make this transition successfully tend to share one characteristic: they decide early that the goal is a platform, not a proof. They invest in the infrastructure layer at the same time as they invest in the model layer — not sequentially, not as a second phase, but concurrently.

This requires a different kind of partnership with the teams delivering the capability. It requires engineers who understand both the model and the production environment. It requires architects who can design for the operational reality of the organisation, not just the technical requirements of the use case. And it requires a shared commitment to a definition of success that includes reliability, maintainability, and operational continuity — not just accuracy on a test dataset.

The AI was proven. The question is always whether the organisation is ready to sustain it.

See how BI3 has bridged this gap

Our Passenger Feedback Intelligence case study is a direct example of taking a proven AI capability from pilot to continuous, enterprise-grade production platform.

Passenger Feedback Intelligence Predictive Airport Operations

Our approach

Leadership that is
accountable for outcomes.

At BI3, leadership is not a title — it is a commitment to delivery. Our directors bring decades of combined experience across enterprise data strategy, AI in production, platform engineering, and global transformation programmes. They have built and led high-performance teams across Australia, Asia, and the Americas, always with accountability for real, measurable results.

We believe the strongest consulting relationships are built on trust, transparency, and the willingness to be judged by what we actually achieve. Our leadership team sets that standard from the top.

CEO & Managing Director
Rodney Agassi
"We don't just consult — we partner with our clients and hold ourselves accountable for outcomes."
About Rodney

Rodney Agassi is the CEO and Managing Director of BI3, bringing over two decades of experience in enterprise data strategy and digital transformation. He has led large-scale programmes across Australia, Asia, and the Americas, with a consistent focus on measurable outcomes and accountable delivery. Before founding BI3, Rodney held senior leadership roles at global consulting firms and enterprise technology organisations, where he shaped data and AI strategies for some of the world's most complex institutions. His experience spans financial services, aviation, government, and infrastructure — working at the intersection of strategy, architecture, and organisational change. Rodney is a frequent speaker at industry events on the topics of enterprise AI adoption, data governance, and the future of intelligent operations. He is known for his belief that sustainable technology transformation is built on trust, transparency, and rigorous accountability to outcomes.

Areas of Expertise
  • Enterprise Data Strategy
  • AI in Production
  • Executive Advisory
  • Global Transformation Programmes

CTO & Co-Founder
Thiru PS
"Agentic AI is about engineering for reliability and scalability — those who master this balance will lead the next wave."
About Rodney

Thiru PS is the Co-Founder and Chief Technology Officer at BI3. With deep expertise in platform engineering, AI systems, and large-scale data infrastructure, Thiru leads the technical direction of BI3's delivery practice and product development. His work spans production AI, real-time analytics, and enterprise-grade platform design. Thiru has spent over fifteen years building and scaling data platforms for global enterprises, with particular depth in agentic AI architectures, streaming data pipelines, and cloud-native infrastructure. He has led technical teams across Southeast Asia, Australia, and the United States, consistently focused on reliability, performance, and the practical challenges of deploying AI at scale. At BI3, Thiru is responsible for engineering standards, technical innovation, and ensuring that every solution the firm delivers meets the rigour required for enterprise production environments. He believes that the difference between a successful AI initiative and a failed one is almost always an engineering problem — and that solving it requires both depth and discipline.

  • Agentic AI Systems
  • Real-Time Data Infrastructure

Chief Operating Officer
Shahnewaz Khan
"The organisation's culture must prioritise data over gut feel — quality data must be accessible to support every strategic decision."
About Rodney

Shahnewaz Khan is the Chief Operating Officer of BI3, responsible for the operational framework that enables the firm to deliver complex, multi-geography programmes with consistency and rigour. He brings a disciplined approach to delivery governance, resource management, and the building of high-performance teams. With a background spanning programme delivery, operational strategy, and data-driven organisational design, Shahnewaz has led large-scale transformation initiatives across aviation, financial services, and public sector clients. He is known for his ability to translate strategic intent into operational reality — creating the structures, processes, and culture that allow ambitious programmes to succeed. At BI3, Shahnewaz oversees the firm's delivery operations globally, working closely with client leadership and internal teams to ensure that execution standards are consistently high. He is a strong advocate for building data-driven cultures within organisations — where decisions are grounded in evidence, performance is transparent, and accountability is shared.

Chief Financial Officer
Michael Tanuhardja
"Sustainable growth demands financial discipline paired with strategic ambition — we invest where the data points."
About Rodney

Michael Tanuhardja is the Chief Financial Officer of BI3, responsible for financial strategy, commercial governance, and sustainable growth planning. He brings a disciplined, data-driven approach to investment decisions and resource allocation, ensuring BI3 scales with rigour and purpose. Michael has extensive experience in corporate finance and commercial leadership within technology and professional services firms, with a particular focus on high-growth environments where capital efficiency and commercial precision are critical to long-term success. He has advised boards and leadership teams on financial structuring, M&A, and the commercial models that support scalable enterprise services. At BI3, Michael plays a central role in shaping how the firm invests in its people, capabilities, and client relationships. He believes that financial discipline is not a constraint on ambition — it is what makes ambition sustainable. His approach combines analytical rigour with a strong understanding of the operational realities that drive long-term value creation.
